results.possibleIdentity: Marilyn Perlman (1925–2010) was an American artist active in the New York and New Jersey art scenes from at least the late 1960s through the 1980s. She was categorized as an 'accomplished artist' and lived in New York City and Upper Montclair, New Jersey. Her professional presence in the art world is documented in the archives of the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), specifically within the records of the Art Lending Service (ALS) and Art Advisory Service (AAS) for the years 1973–1974. These services were designed to promote contemporary art to collectors and corporations. In 1985, she held an exhibition titled 'Continuous Heavens,' which ran from June 29 to August 18. Her work and exhibition materials are preserved in the archives of art critics Nicolas and Elena Calas. She had three children: Meg Perlman (a prominent art curator and former director of the Pollock-Krasner House), Noah Perlman, and Beth Perlman. Her career aligns with the active period of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), and her inclusion in MoMA's advisory files and association with critics like Calas places her within the same avant-garde circles that collaborated with the organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Cyril "Bob" Slosson (1925–2006) was an aeronautical engineer who may be the individual associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) due to the organization's focus on collaborations between artists and engineers. A graduate of the University of Kansas (Class of 1951), he worked as an aeronautical engineer for McDonnell Douglas for 25 years, a period coinciding with the peak activity of E.A.T. in the late 1960s and early 1970s. While search results do not explicitly categorize him as a fine artist, E.A.T. frequently credited engineers as collaborative partners in the creation of technological artworks. He was also a WWII veteran and lived in the St. Louis, Missouri area, which hosted an active E.A.T. chapter. Another individual by the same name was an aerospace machinist at Boeing in the Pacific Northwest during the same era, another region with significant E.A.T. activity.

Edwin Emery Slosson (1865–1929) was a chemist and science popularizer whose writings on the intersection of science, technology, and culture were featured in the landmark 1968 Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). Although he was not active as an artist during the late 1960s or early 1970s (having died in 1929), his intellectual contributions were utilized by E.A.T. to provide historical and philosophical context for the collaboration between the arts and sciences. The name 'Bob Slosson' may be a misidentification of Edwin Slosson or a reference to a contemporary relative associated with his legacy.
